subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Close your eyes for a moment. Everything goes dark, doesn't it? Now open them again and look around. In an instant you can see your hands, the room, the colour of the walls, and the bright sky outside the window. Something amazing has just happened, and it happens every single time you open your eyes. That amazing thing is light. In this unit, you are going to become a light detective. You will discover where light comes from, how it travels, why we can see, why shadows appear, and why a mirror can show you your own face.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain that we see an object when light bounces off it and travels into our eyes.
Tell the difference between a light source (something that makes its own light) and an object that only reflects light.
Sort light sources into natural ones (like the Sun) and artificial ones (like a torch) that people make.
Explain that light travels in straight lines and use this to explain how shadows form.
Describe how reflection works and why a smooth, shiny surface reflects better than a rough one.
Sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by how they let light through.
Explain in simple words how your eye detects light so that you can see.
Follow safety rules that protect your eyes when you work with light.
Here are the important words for this unit. Read each one slowly. You will meet all of them again as you go, so do not worry about memorising them all at once.

concepts.title: Light — part 1
concepts.explanation: Close your eyes for a moment. Everything goes dark, doesn't it? Now open them again and look around. In an instant you can see your hands, the room, the colour of the walls, and the bright sky outside the window. Something amazing has just happened, and it happens every single time you open your eyes. That amazing thing is light. In this unit, you are going to become a light detective. You will discover where light comes from, how it travels, why we can see, why shadows appear, and why a mirror can show you your…
concepts.example: Think about a bright morning in Mogadishu or Hargeisa. The Sun climbs over the horizon and pours golden light across the land. The equatorial Sun near the East African coast is very strong and very bright, much stronger…

concepts.title: Light — part 2
concepts.explanation: Think about a bright morning in Mogadishu or Hargeisa. The Sun climbs over the horizon and pours golden light across the land. The equatorial Sun near the East African coast is very strong and very bright, much stronger than in cooler, cloudier countries far from the equator. That strong sunshine lets you see the acacia trees, the goats searching for grass, the flamingos standing in a shallow lake, and the waves rolling onto the Indian Ocean shore. Without light, you would see none of it. Light is one of Allah's…
concepts.example: You will learn best if you become an active investigator, not just a reader. When this book asks you to look at something, actually look. When it asks you to try something with a torch or a mirror, actually try it. Keep…

concepts.title: Light — part 3
concepts.explanation: You will learn best if you become an active investigator, not just a reader. When this book asks you to look at something, actually look. When it asks you to try something with a torch or a mirror, actually try it. Keep a notebook beside you and write down what you notice. Science is not just words on a page. Science is careful looking, careful thinking, and careful testing.
concepts.example: Ask Your AI Tutor Say to your tutor: "I am starting Unit 5 about Light. Can you ask me three questions to find out what I already know about how we see things?" Your AI tutor is available any time of the day or night,…

concepts.title: Light — part 4
concepts.explanation: Ask Your AI Tutor Say to your tutor: "I am starting Unit 5 about Light. Can you ask me three questions to find out what I already know about how we see things?" Your AI tutor is available any time of the day or night, so ask it whenever you feel curious or stuck.
concepts.example: Light is not only about seeing. Light keeps you alive and healthy in many ways. The Sun's light helps plants grow, and those plants feed the goats, cattle, and camels that give your family milk and meat. Sunlight helps…

concepts.title: Light — part 5
concepts.explanation: Light is not only about seeing. Light keeps you alive and healthy in many ways. The Sun's light helps plants grow, and those plants feed the goats, cattle, and camels that give your family milk and meat. Sunlight helps your own body make something it needs to keep your bones strong. Farmers watch the light of sunrise and sunset to plan their day. Fishermen along the Kismaayo and Mombasa coast read the light on the water. When night falls and the light disappears, families light a lamp, a torch, or a cooking fire…
concepts.example: Understanding light also keeps you safe. You will learn a very important safety rule in this unit: never look straight at the Sun, because its light is so powerful it can damage your eyes forever. You will also learn…

concepts.title: Light — part 6
concepts.explanation: Understanding light also keeps you safe. You will learn a very important safety rule in this unit: never look straight at the Sun, because its light is so powerful it can damage your eyes forever. You will also learn why you should never shine a torch straight into someone's eyes, and never use a mirror to flash bright sunlight into a person's face. A good scientist is also a careful and kind person.
concepts.example: Close your eyes for a moment. Everything goes dark, doesn't it? Now open them again and look around. In an instant you can see your hands, the room, the colour of the walls, and the bright sky outside the window.…

explorations.title: Can You See in the Dark?
explorations.context: To find out whether we can see objects when there is no light at all. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down what you think. Do you think you will be able to see the objects in a completely dark room? Why or why not?
explorations.prompt: Could you see the objects in the dark? What does this tell you about light and seeing?
explorations.answer: Could you see the objects in the dark? What does this tell you about light and seeing? The objects never moved. So why could you not see them in the dark? What had to be present before you could see again?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out whether we can see objects when there is no light at all. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down what you think. Do you think you will be able to see the objects in a completely dark room? Why or why not?

explorations.title: Light Travels in Straight Lines
explorations.context: To show that light travels in straight lines and cannot bend around corners. Make a Prediction If you make three cards with a hole in each, do you think you will see the torch only when all three holes line up in a straight line?
explorations.prompt: When could you see the torch light: when the holes were lined up, or when they were not?
explorations.answer: When could you see the torch light: when the holes were lined up, or when they were not? Why could you not see the light when the middle hole was moved out of line? What does this tell you about the path light takes?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show that light travels in straight lines and cannot bend around corners. Make a Prediction If you make three cards with a hole in each, do you think you will see the torch only when all three holes line up in a straight line?

explorations.title: Making and Changing Shadows
explorations.context: To investigate how the size of a shadow changes when you move an object closer to or further from a light. Make a Prediction What do you think happens to a shadow when you move the object closer to the torch? Does it get bigger or smaller?
explorations.prompt: When was the shadow biggest? When was it smallest?
explorations.answer: When was the shadow biggest? When was it smallest? Why do you think moving the object nearer the torch changed the shadow size? Why does the object make a shadow at all? (Think about the word opaque.)
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To investigate how the size of a shadow changes when you move an object closer to or further from a light. Make a Prediction What do you think happens to a shadow when you move the object closer to the torch? Does it get bigger or smaller?

explorations.title: Smooth and Rough Reflectors
explorations.context: To find out which surfaces reflect light best and give the clearest picture. Make a Prediction Which do you think will give the clearest reflection: a mirror, a shiny spoon, kitchen foil, white paper, or a rough cloth? Put them in order before you start.
explorations.prompt: Which surfaces gave the clearest reflection? What did they have in common?
explorations.answer: Which surfaces gave the clearest reflection? What did they have in common? Why did the crumpled foil give a worse reflection than the smooth foil, even though it is the same material? Use the words smooth, rough, and scatter to explain your results.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which surfaces reflect light best and give the clearest picture. Make a Prediction Which do you think will give the clearest reflection: a mirror, a shiny spoon, kitchen foil, white paper, or a rough cloth? Put them in order before you start.

explorations.title: Transparent, Translucent, or Opaque?
explorations.context: To sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by testing how light passes through them. Make a Prediction Look at your materials and predict which will be transparent (clear), translucent (blurry), and opaque (blocks light).
explorations.prompt: Which materials were transparent? How could you tell?
explorations.answer: Which materials were transparent? How could you tell? Which materials were opaque? What do opaque materials do to light? Which opaque material would make the darkest, clearest shadow, and why?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by testing how light passes through them. Make a Prediction Look at your materials and predict which will be transparent (clear), translucent (blurry), and opaque (blocks light).

methods.title: Can You See in the Dark?
methods.example: To find out whether we can see objects when there is no light at all. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down what you think. Do you think you will be able to see the objects in a completely dark room? Why or why not?
steps: Place the three objects on a table and look at them in normal light. Note their colours.
With an adult's help, make the room as dark as you safely can. Close curtains and doors.
Wait one minute, then try to see the three objects. Write down exactly what you can and cannot see.
Now switch on the torch and shine it towards the objects (not towards anyone's eyes).
Write down what you can see now.

methods.title: Light Travels in Straight Lines
methods.example: To show that light travels in straight lines and cannot bend around corners. Make a Prediction If you make three cards with a hole in each, do you think you will see the torch only when all three holes line up in a straight line?
steps: Make a small hole in the centre of each card, all at the same height.
Stand the three cards in a row on a table, one behind the other, so the holes line up in a straight line.
In a dim room, shine the torch through the first hole. Look through the last card. Can you see the light?
Now slide the middle card sideways so its hole is no longer in line with the others.
Shine the torch again and look. Can you still see the light through the holes?

methods.title: Making and Changing Shadows
methods.example: To investigate how the size of a shadow changes when you move an object closer to or further from a light. Make a Prediction What do you think happens to a shadow when you move the object closer to the torch? Does it get bigger or smaller?
steps: In a dim room, hold the torch still and shine it at the wall.
Hold the object halfway between the torch and the wall so it makes a shadow on the wall.
Measure the height of the shadow and write it down.
Move the object closer to the torch. Measure the shadow again.
Move the object closer to the wall. Measure the shadow again.

methods.title: Smooth and Rough Reflectors
methods.example: To find out which surfaces reflect light best and give the clearest picture. Make a Prediction Which do you think will give the clearest reflection: a mirror, a shiny spoon, kitchen foil, white paper, or a rough cloth? Put them in order before you start.
steps: In a dim room, hold each item up one at a time and try to see your face or the room in it.
Shine the torch gently onto each surface (never towards eyes) and watch how the light bounces.
Give each surface a score from 1 (no clear reflection) to 5 (very clear reflection).
Now compare the smooth foil with the crumpled foil.

methods.title: Transparent, Translucent, or Opaque?
methods.example: To sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by testing how light passes through them. Make a Prediction Look at your materials and predict which will be transparent (clear), translucent (blurry), and opaque (blocks light).
steps: In a dim room, hold each material in front of the torch, one at a time.
Shine the torch through the material towards a wall (not towards eyes).
Decide: does all the light pass through (transparent), some blurry light (translucent), or no light (opaque)?
Try to see a shape or your fingers through each material.

workedExamples.title: Measuring a Shadow
workedExamples.prompt: Some light questions involve simple measuring. Let us work through one together, step by step. Amina wants to find out how the shadow of a stick changes as the Sun moves. She pushes a straight stick into the ground and measures the length of its shadow at…
workedExamples.solution: She writes down her results: Shadow length Where the Sun is

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a natural light source? (a) a torch; (b) an electric bulb; (c) the Sun; (d) a mirror.
workedExamples.solution: 2: (c) The Sun is natural. Torches and bulbs are made by people, so they are artificial. A mirror is not a source at all.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a light source? (a) the Moon; (b) a mirror; (c) a candle flame; (d) a white wall.
workedExamples.solution: 3: (c) A candle flame makes its own light. The Moon, a mirror, and a white wall only reflect light.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Light travels in: (a) curved paths; (b) straight lines; (c) circles; (d) zig-zags on its own.
workedExamples.solution: 4: (b) Light travels in straight lines. This is why shadows form.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: A shadow forms because an object: (a) makes darkness; (b) blocks light that travels in straight lines; (c) absorbs the Sun; (d) reflects light.
workedExamples.solution: 5: (b) A shadow forms when an opaque object blocks straight-travelling light, so a dark area is left behind it.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which surface gives the clearest reflection? (a) rough cloth; (b) a smooth mirror; (c) crumpled foil; (d) dry sand.
workedExamples.solution: 6: (b) A smooth mirror reflects light in an orderly way and gives the clearest image. Rough surfaces scatter light.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: A material you can see through clearly is called: (a) opaque; (b) translucent; (c) transparent; (d) reflective.
workedExamples.solution: 7: (c) Transparent materials let light through clearly so you can see through them.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Frosted glass and a thin curtain are examples of: (a) transparent materials; (b) translucent materials; (c) opaque materials; (d) light sources.
workedExamples.solution: 8: (b) Translucent materials let some light through but scatter it, so the view is blurry.

practice.prompt: We can see objects that do not make their own light because: (a) our eyes send out beams; (b) light reflects off them into our eyes; (c) the objects glow; (d) our brain imagines them.
practice.answer: 1: (b) Light reflects off objects into our eyes. Our eyes do not send out beams; they catch light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a natural light source? (a) a torch; (b) an electric bulb; (c) the Sun; (d) a mirror.
practice.answer: 2: (c) The Sun is natural. Torches and bulbs are made by people, so they are artificial. A mirror is not a source at all.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a light source? (a) the Moon; (b) a mirror; (c) a candle flame; (d) a white wall.
practice.answer: 3: (c) A candle flame makes its own light. The Moon, a mirror, and a white wall only reflect light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Light travels in: (a) curved paths; (b) straight lines; (c) circles; (d) zig-zags on its own.
practice.answer: 4: (b) Light travels in straight lines. This is why shadows form.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A shadow forms because an object: (a) makes darkness; (b) blocks light that travels in straight lines; (c) absorbs the Sun; (d) reflects light.
practice.answer: 5: (b) A shadow forms when an opaque object blocks straight-travelling light, so a dark area is left behind it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which surface gives the clearest reflection? (a) rough cloth; (b) a smooth mirror; (c) crumpled foil; (d) dry sand.
practice.answer: 6: (b) A smooth mirror reflects light in an orderly way and gives the clearest image. Rough surfaces scatter light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A material you can see through clearly is called: (a) opaque; (b) translucent; (c) transparent; (d) reflective.
practice.answer: 7: (c) Transparent materials let light through clearly so you can see through them.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Frosted glass and a thin curtain are examples of: (a) transparent materials; (b) translucent materials; (c) opaque materials; (d) light sources.
practice.answer: 8: (b) Translucent materials let some light through but scatter it, so the view is blurry.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The part of the eye that detects light at the back is the: (a) pupil; (b) cornea; (c) lens; (d) retina.
practice.answer: 9: (d) The retina at the back of the eye detects light and sends a signal to the brain.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Your shadow is longest when the Sun is: (a) high overhead at midday; (b) low near the horizon; (c) hidden by clouds; (d) behind a wall.
practice.answer: 10: (b) When the Sun is low near the horizon, light comes in at a slant and shadows are long.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The Moon looks bright at night because it: (a) makes its own light; (b) reflects sunlight; (c) is on fire; (d) is a star.
practice.answer: 11: (b) The Moon reflects sunlight; it does not make its own light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is the correct safety rule? (a) stare at the Sun to test your eyes; (b) never look directly at the Sun; (c) shine torches in eyes; (d) flash mirrors at faces.
practice.answer: 12: (b) Never look directly at the Sun, because it can permanently damage your eyes.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Can You See in the Dark?
activities.materials: A small room you can make very dark (a room with thick curtains, or a large cupboard with an adult's permission); Three small objects of different colours, such as a red cup, a spoon, and a cloth; A torch
steps: Place the three objects on a table and look at them in normal light. Note their colours.
With an adult's help, make the room as dark as you safely can. Close curtains and doors.
Wait one minute, then try to see the three objects. Write down exactly what you can and cannot see.
Now switch on the torch and shine it towards the objects (not towards anyone's eyes).
Write down what you can see now.

activities.title: Light Travels in Straight Lines
activities.materials: Three pieces of card or stiff paper of the same size; A pencil and a small coin to draw and cut equal holes (ask an adult to help with cutting); Some lumps of clay, dough, or tape to stand the cards up; A torch
steps: Make a small hole in the centre of each card, all at the same height.
Stand the three cards in a row on a table, one behind the other, so the holes line up in a straight line.
In a dim room, shine the torch through the first hole. Look through the last card. Can you see the light?
Now slide the middle card sideways so its hole is no longer in line with the others.
Shine the torch again and look. Can you still see the light through the holes?

activities.title: Making and Changing Shadows
activities.materials: A torch; A small opaque object, such as a plastic toy, a spoon, or a small cup; A light-coloured wall or a sheet of white paper taped to a wall; A ruler or measuring tape
steps: In a dim room, hold the torch still and shine it at the wall.
Hold the object halfway between the torch and the wall so it makes a shadow on the wall.
Measure the height of the shadow and write it down.
Move the object closer to the torch. Measure the shadow again.
Move the object closer to the wall. Measure the shadow again.

activities.title: Smooth and Rough Reflectors
activities.materials: A mirror (ask an adult if it is glass); A clean, shiny metal spoon; A piece of smooth kitchen foil and a piece of crumpled foil; A sheet of white paper; A rough cloth or piece of cardboard; A torch
steps: In a dim room, hold each item up one at a time and try to see your face or the room in it.
Shine the torch gently onto each surface (never towards eyes) and watch how the light bounces.
Give each surface a score from 1 (no clear reflection) to 5 (very clear reflection).
Now compare the smooth foil with the crumpled foil.

activities.title: Transparent, Translucent, or Opaque?
activities.materials: A torch; Clear plastic or a clean clear bottle; A thin cloth or thin paper; Wax paper or a thin plastic bag; Cardboard, wood, or a metal lid; A cup of clean water
steps: In a dim room, hold each material in front of the torch, one at a time.
Shine the torch through the material towards a wall (not towards eyes).
Decide: does all the light pass through (transparent), some blurry light (translucent), or no light (opaque)?
Try to see a shape or your fingers through each material.

activities.title: Observe: Light — part 6
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of light — part 6 around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: A shadow forms on the OPPOSITE side of an object from the light source. If light could bend around objects, shadows would be impossible. Shadows are proof that light travels in straight lines.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: Smooth and shiny surfaces (mirror, still water, polished metal) reflect light in an orderly way and make a clear image. Rough or dull surfaces (a wall, cloth, choppy water, sand) scatter light in all directions and make no clear image.

terms: A light source made by people (torch, bulb, candle, phone).
When light bounces off a surface.
A straight line showing the path of light.
A dark area where an opaque object has blocked the light.
Lets light pass straight through; you can see clearly (glass, water).
Lets some light through but scatters it; the view is blurry (frosted glass, thin curtain).
Lets no light through; blocks the light (wood, metal, cardboard).
The eyes and brain working together so you can see.

vocabulary.example: Sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by how they let light through.
Sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by how they let light through.
Sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by how they let light through.

vocabulary.meaning: A light source made by people (torch, bulb, candle, phone).
When light bounces off a surface.
vocabulary.example: Describe how reflection works and why a smooth, shiny surface reflects better than a rough one.

vocabulary.meaning: A straight line showing the path of light.
vocabulary.example: A ray of light travels in and hits the smooth mirror.

vocabulary.meaning: A dark area where an opaque object has blocked the light.
vocabulary.example: An acacia tree casts a long shadow on the ground at sunset.

vocabulary.meaning: Lets light pass straight through; you can see clearly (glass, water).
vocabulary.example: Sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by how they let light through.

vocabulary.meaning: Lets some light through but scatters it; the view is blurry (frosted glass, thin curtain).
vocabulary.example: Sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by how they let light through.

vocabulary.meaning: Lets no light through; blocks the light (wood, metal, cardboard).
vocabulary.example: Sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by how they let light through.

vocabulary.meaning: The eyes and brain working together so you can see.
vocabulary.example: You use vision to read these words right now.

commonMistakes: Saying the Moon is a light source.
The Moon makes no light of its own.
Thinking eyes make light so we can see in the dark.
Eyes only catch light; they do not make it.
Thinking light bends around corners by itself.
Light travels only in straight lines.
Saying a shadow is dark light the object gives off.
Objects do not give off shadows.

connections.text: Light is a form of energy, like heat and electricity. Solar panels change light energy into electrical energy.
Plants need sunlight to grow, and our eyes let us see the world around us.
Measuring shadow lengths and reflection uses rulers, tables, and comparing numbers.
The strong equatorial Sun, the seasons, and day and night all connect to light.
Artists use light, shadow, and reflection to make pictures look real.
Watch how your shadow changes from morning to evening and keep a simple diary.

fluency.prompt: A material you can see through clearly is called: (a) opaque; (b) translucent; (c) transparent; (d) reflective.
fluency.answer: 7: (c) Transparent materials let light through clearly so you can see through them.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 7: (c) Transparent materials let light through clearly so you can see through them.

fluency.prompt: Frosted glass and a thin curtain are examples of: (a) transparent materials; (b) translucent materials; (c) opaque materials; (d) light sources.
fluency.answer: 8: (b) Translucent materials let some light through but scatter it, so the view is blurry.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 8: (b) Translucent materials let some light through but scatter it, so the view is blurry.

fluency.prompt: The part of the eye that detects light at the back is the: (a) pupil; (b) cornea; (c) lens; (d) retina.
fluency.answer: 9: (d) The retina at the back of the eye detects light and sends a signal to the brain.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 9: (d) The retina at the back of the eye detects light and sends a signal to the brain.

fluency.prompt: Your shadow is longest when the Sun is: (a) high overhead at midday; (b) low near the horizon; (c) hidden by clouds; (d) behind a wall.
fluency.answer: 10: (b) When the Sun is low near the horizon, light comes in at a slant and shadows are long.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10: (b) When the Sun is low near the horizon, light comes in at a slant and shadows are long.

fluency.prompt: The Moon looks bright at night because it: (a) makes its own light; (b) reflects sunlight; (c) is on fire; (d) is a star.
fluency.answer: 11: (b) The Moon reflects sunlight; it does not make its own light.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11: (b) The Moon reflects sunlight; it does not make its own light.

fluency.prompt: Which is the correct safety rule? (a) stare at the Sun to test your eyes; (b) never look directly at the Sun; (c) shine torches in eyes; (d) flash mirrors at faces.
fluency.answer: 12: (b) Never look directly at the Sun, because it can permanently damage your eyes.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: 1. Suitable answers: a torch, a candle, or an oil lamp. She needs a light source because without light there is nothing to reflect off the page into her eyes, so she could not see to read.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. Suitable answers: a torch, a candle, or an oil lamp. She needs a light source because without light there is nothing to reflect off the page into her eyes, so she could not see to read.

realProblems.prompt: Amina wants to read a book after dark in Hargeisa. There is no electricity that evening. Suggest two artificial light sources she could use, and explain why she needs a light source at all.
realProblems.answer: 2. The solar panel catches the Sun's natural light during the day and stores its energy. After dark, that stored energy powers the electric bulb, an artificial source. So a natural source ends up lighting the home at night.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2. The solar panel catches the Sun's natural light during the day and stores its energy. After dark, that stored energy powers the electric bulb, an artificial source. So a natural source ends up lighting the home at night.

realProblems.prompt: A family near the coast puts solar panels on their roof to catch the strong equatorial Sun. Explain how a natural light source (the Sun) ends up powering an artificial light source (a bulb) at night.
realProblems.answer: 3. When the pond was still and smooth, it reflected light in an orderly way and made a clear picture. Stirring made the surface rough, which scattered the light in all directions, so the clear picture disappeared.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3. When the pond was still and smooth, it reflected light in an orderly way and made a clear picture. Stirring made the surface rough, which scattered the light in all directions, so the clear picture disappeared.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan looks into a calm pond after the rains and sees the sky and his own face. His little sister stirs the water with a stick and the picture disappears. Explain to his sister what happened, using the words smooth, rough, and scatter.
realProblems.answer: 4. A translucent curtain is best. A clear glass window alone gives no privacy, and an opaque board blocks all daylight. A translucent curtain lets soft daylight in while stopping people from seeing clearly inside.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4. A translucent curtain is best. A clear glass window alone gives no privacy, and an opaque board blocks all daylight. A translucent curtain lets soft daylight in while stopping people from seeing clearly inside.

realProblems.prompt: Khadija wants privacy in her room but also wants some soft daylight to come in. Would a clear glass window, a translucent curtain, or an opaque wooden board be best? Explain your choice.
realProblems.answer: 5. Flashing bright sunlight into someone's eyes can dazzle and even damage them. Concentrated light is dangerous, so we never reflect the Sun into anyone's face.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5. Flashing bright sunlight into someone's eyes can dazzle and even damage them. Concentrated light is dangerous, so we never reflect the Sun into anyone's face.

realProblems.prompt: During a game, Omar is about to flash sunlight off a mirror into his friend's face for fun. Explain why he must not do this.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why you cannot see anything in a completely dark room.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. In complete darkness there is no light to reflect off objects into your eyes, so you see nothing. Objects are still there, but without light there is no seeing.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a light source and a reflector? Give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. A light source makes its own light (for example, the Sun or a torch). A reflector only bounces back light that came from somewhere else (for example, the Moon or a mirror).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain, in three steps, how you are able to see a goat standing in a field.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. Step 1: light from a source (like the Sun) travels to the goat. Step 2: the light reflects off the goat. Step 3: the reflected light enters your eyes, so you see the goat.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does an opaque object make a shadow?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. An opaque object does not let light through, so it blocks the straight-travelling light. The space behind it stays dark, and that dark area is the shadow.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why can you see your face clearly in still water but not in choppy water?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. Still water is smooth, so it reflects light in an orderly way and gives a clear image. Choppy water is rough, so it scatters the light in all directions and the picture breaks up.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three groups we sort materials into by how they let light through, and give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. Transparent (clear glass, water), translucent (thin curtain, frosted glass), and opaque (wood, metal).

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can explain that we see an object when light bounces off it and travels into our eyes.
I can tell the difference between a light source (something that makes its own light) and an object that only reflects light.
I can sort light sources into natural ones (like the Sun) and artificial ones (like a torch) that people make.
I can explain that light travels in straight lines and use this to explain how shadows form.
I can describe how reflection works and why a smooth, shiny surface reflects better than a rough one.
I can sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by how they let light through.
I can explain in simple words how your eye detects light so that you can see.
